prompt: We're migrating to an event-driven architecture. Our architect says this will make our services loosely coupled. How should I evaluate that claim?
expected: Skill activates and interrogates the 'loosely coupled' claim across the coupling dimensions: the big decoupling step is RPC→async messaging (temporal/location/space), while Pub-Sub's only unique win over P2P is topology coupling. It stresses that data-format and semantic coupling stay tight regardless, so 'EDA' is not a synonym for decoupled — ask which specific dimension the design reduces.

prompt: We publish an OrderPlaced event on SNS and fan out to 14 downstream services. We renamed a field last week and had to update 11 of those services. Why is our 'loosely coupled' event bus causing so much coordination?
expected: Skill activates and diagnoses the Pub-Sub growth trap: the bus decoupled topology (adding subscribers is free) but every subscriber remains data-format and semantically coupled to the schema, so left-to-right schema changes propagate to all of them. It explains this is expected behavior, not a bus bug, and points toward schema governance/versioning (and potentially a canonical data model) to manage evolution.

prompt: We use AWS EventBridge as our event bus. When I add a new consumer, I have to add a rule target. Is this real Pub-Sub or something else?
expected: Skill activates and identifies the EventBridge trap: adding a rule target means it behaves as a Recipient List, not a true Pub-Sub channel. The producer is untouched, but modifying a central rule reintroduces right-to-left coupling — the topology-decoupling benefit is displaced to the broker configuration rather than eliminated.

prompt: Our team is debating whether to name our messages as events (OrderPlaced, UserRegistered) versus commands (ProcessOrder, CreateUser). Does the naming choice affect how decoupled the system is?
expected: Skill activates and clarifies that event-vs-command naming (semantics) changes nothing about coupling — sender and receiver must still agree on field names, types, and meaning either way. What actually affects decoupling is the channel type (Pub-Sub vs. point-to-point), not the message name.

prompt: We receive events from an AWS service (S3 bucket notifications). Should we use Pub-Sub or point-to-point for our internal consumers? We don't control the S3 event format.
expected: Skill activates and applies the uncontrolled-source rule: because you don't control S3's producer, Pub-Sub's right-to-left decoupling is its most valuable property — the AWS team will never change their service for you, so a Pub-Sub channel lets you add internal consumers freely without touching the source. It recommends Pub-Sub here.

prompt: How does switching from REST APIs to an event bus change the coupling between our microservices? We want to understand concretely what gets better and what stays the same.
expected: Skill activates and runs the coupling comparison table dimension by dimension: switching RPC→async messaging improves temporal, location, and space coupling (sender no longer blocks or hardcodes the receiver); Pub-Sub additionally decouples topology. What stays the same is data-format and semantic coupling — consumers still depend on the schema — so those must still be governed.

prompt: We have 40 microservices all subscribing to a shared CustomerUpdated event. We need to add a new field to it. What are we dealing with here?
expected: Skill activates and names this the Pub-Sub growth trap: the 40 subscribers are topology-decoupled (easy to add) but all data-format coupled to the schema, so evolving the event is now a coordination burden across every subscriber. It advises additive/backward-compatible schema changes and schema governance rather than treating it as a bus failure.

prompt: A vendor is pitching us their event mesh solution, claiming it will decouple all our services. What questions should I ask to evaluate that claim?
expected: Skill activates and supplies the dimensional interrogation: which specific coupling dimension does the mesh reduce, is it true Pub-Sub or a managed Recipient List (does adding a consumer require a central config change?), and how does it address the data-format/semantic coupling and schema-evolution growth trap it leaves untouched. It rejects the blanket 'decouples all services' claim as a style label.

prompt: How do I configure dead letter queues on AWS SQS for messages that fail processing?
What is the difference between a topic and a queue in Apache Kafka?
We need to process 10,000 messages per second from SQS. How many consumers should we run?
Should I use REST or gRPC for communication between two services in the same Kubernetes cluster?
How do I implement idempotency in my message consumer to handle duplicate deliveries?
We have microservices A and B. A publishes an event; B subscribes. Someone says we're 'decoupled.' What does that actually mean and is it true?
expected: Skill activates and gives the richer diagnosis rather than a simple Pub-Sub explanation: A and B are temporally/location decoupled and topology-decoupled (more subscribers can be added without touching A), but they remain data-format and semantically coupled through the shared event schema. The defensible answer is 'partly true — decoupled on some dimensions, still coupled on others,' naming which.

prompt: We're choosing between calling our downstream services synchronously via REST versus publishing an event and letting them subscribe. Which approach gives us better decoupling?
expected: Skill activates and uses the coupling comparison table as the deciding tool: the event/Pub-Sub option removes temporal, location, and topology coupling that REST imposes, so it gives better decoupling on those axes — but both leave data-format and semantic coupling intact. The reasoning it surfaces is to decide on dimensions and source-control, not on the 'REST vs events' style label; it may note this also overlaps integration-style selection.
